title: Disease burden from non-communicable diseases vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: Disease burden to non-communicable diseases (NCDs), measured in DALYs (Disability-Adjusted Life Years) per 100,000 individuals versus gross domestic product (GDP) per capita, adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Disease burden from non-communicable diseases vs. GDP per capita, 2023

Disease burden to non-communicable diseases (NCDs), measured in DALYs (Disability-Adjusted Life Years) per 100,000 individuals versus gross domestic product (GDP) per capita, adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

The scatter plot maps the relationship between national wealth and health outcomes by plotting Disability-Adjusted Life Years (DALYs) per 100,000 people against GDP per capita in international dollars on a logarithmic scale. The vertical axis ranges from 0 to 50,000 DALYs, while the horizontal axis spans from under $2,000 to over $100,000 in GDP per capita. A negative relationship is visible across the dataset, where higher income levels generally coincide with lower disease burdens from non-communicable diseases. The highest values for disease burden appear in the lower-left quadrant, occupied largely by countries in Africa and Oceania. Nauru sits at the top of the chart with a disease burden near 50,000 DALYs per 100,000, while the Solomon Islands and Marshall Islands also register values above 40,000. Other nations with high burdens relative to their income include Haiti, Myanmar, and several sub-Saharan African countries such as Burundi, DR Congo, and Niger, all positioned above the 25,000 DALY mark. In the middle of the distribution, countries like India, Indonesia, and Vietnam show moderate disease burdens between 15,000 and 25,000 DALYs with GDP per capita figures ranging from $5,000 to $15,000. The lowest disease burdens cluster in the bottom-right section of the chart, corresponding to high-income economies. Singapore, Japan, and Germany display the lowest values, falling below 15,000 DALYs per 100,000, with GDP per capita exceeding $40,000. The United States and Guyana appear as outliers in the upper-right area, showing higher disease burdens than other wealthy nations despite high GDP per capita. Circle sizes indicate population, with large circles for India and China visible in the middle-income range. The data shows a clear downward trend where increased economic output per person aligns with reduced years of life lost or lived with disability due to non-communicable conditions.
